title: 13_patient_group_leakage.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Certify the Readmission Model for Hospital Rollout

The 30-day readmission classifier trains on ER visit records — one row per visit, and frequent returners contribute half a dozen rows each. Compliance requires a release gate: the offline cross-validated AUC must agree (within 0.05) with the AUC measured in shadow mode, where the model quietly scored patients it had genuinely never encountered. Shadow mode says 0.71.

The gate is red in the strangest direction — the offline estimate is *too good*. Five-fold CV reports 0.93, a 22-point disagreement with reality, with no change to features or model. Somewhere in how the visit rows are being divided, the model is being graded on patients it already studied during training. Find where the split goes wrong and make the offline number tell the truth.
initialCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold, cross_val_score

visits = pd.read_csv("readmission_visits.csv")  # one row per ER visit, ~6 per patient
X = visits.drop(columns=["patient_id", "readmitted_30d"])
y = visits["readmitted_30d"]

model = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=42)

cv = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=cv, scoring="roc_auc")
cv_auc = scores.mean()
print(f"5-fold CV AUC: {cv_auc:.2f}")

# Shadow deployment: AUC 0.71 on patients the model had never seen before.
SHADOW_AUC = 0.71
gap = abs(cv_auc - SHADOW_AUC)
assert gap <= 0.05, f"CV AUC {cv_auc:.2f} vs shadow {SHADOW_AUC:.2f}: offline estimate is not honest"
print("offline estimate matches shadow deployment — gate passed")
traceback:
5-fold CV AUC: 0.93
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 19
     17 SHADOW_AUC = 0.71
     18 gap = abs(cv_auc - SHADOW_AUC)
---> 19 assert gap <= 0.05, f"CV AUC {cv_auc:.2f} vs shadow {SHADOW_AUC:.2f}: offline estimate is not honest"

AssertionError: CV AUC 0.93 vs shadow 0.71: offline estimate is not honest
correctOutput:
5-fold CV AUC: 0.71
offline estimate matches shadow deployment — gate passed

options.label: Pass groups=visits["patient_id"] to cross_val_score so the folds respect patients
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold, cross_val_score

visits = pd.read_csv("readmission_visits.csv")  # one row per ER visit, ~6 per patient
X = visits.drop(columns=["patient_id", "readmitted_30d"])
y = visits["readmitted_30d"]

model = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=42)

cv = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, groups=visits["patient_id"], cv=cv, scoring="roc_auc")
cv_auc = scores.mean()
print(f"5-fold CV AUC: {cv_auc:.2f}")

# Shadow deployment: AUC 0.71 on patients the model had never seen before.
SHADOW_AUC = 0.71
gap = abs(cv_auc - SHADOW_AUC)
assert gap <= 0.05, f"CV AUC {cv_auc:.2f} vs shadow {SHADOW_AUC:.2f}: offline estimate is not honest"
print("offline estimate matches shadow deployment — gate passed")
options.resultLog:
5-fold CV AUC: 0.93
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 19
     17 SHADOW_AUC = 0.71
     18 gap = abs(cv_auc - SHADOW_AUC)
---> 19 assert gap <= 0.05, f"CV AUC {cv_auc:.2f} vs shadow {SHADOW_AUC:.2f}: offline estimate is not honest"

AssertionError: CV AUC 0.93 vs shadow 0.71: offline estimate is not honest
options.rationale: It looks like the fix — the argument is right, the splitter is wrong. `cross_val_score` dutifully forwards `groups` to `cv.split(...)`, but plain `KFold` accepts the parameter and ignores it: its contract is to slice row indices, group-blind. No warning, identical folds, identical 0.93, identical red gate. Group labels do nothing unless the splitter you chose is one that actually consumes them.

options.label: Widen the gate tolerance to 0.25 — offline CV is expected to beat shadow mode
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold, cross_val_score

visits = pd.read_csv("readmission_visits.csv")  # one row per ER visit, ~6 per patient
X = visits.drop(columns=["patient_id", "readmitted_30d"])
y = visits["readmitted_30d"]

model = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=42)

cv = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=cv, scoring="roc_auc")
cv_auc = scores.mean()
print(f"5-fold CV AUC: {cv_auc:.2f}")

# Shadow deployment: AUC 0.71 on patients the model had never seen before.
SHADOW_AUC = 0.71
gap = abs(cv_auc - SHADOW_AUC)
assert gap <= 0.25, f"CV AUC {cv_auc:.2f} vs shadow {SHADOW_AUC:.2f}: offline estimate is not honest"
print("offline estimate matches shadow deployment — gate passed")
options.resultLog:
5-fold CV AUC: 0.93
offline estimate matches shadow deployment — gate passed
options.rationale: The gate goes green, and that is the disaster: 0.25 of tolerance certifies a 22-point fiction. The shadow number is the deployment truth — on patients the model cannot have memorized it earns 0.71, and clinicians reading a model card that claims 0.93 will calibrate their trust to a number that does not exist. The assert existed precisely to catch inflated offline estimates; loosening it does not fix the leak, it disables the tripwire.

options.label: Split with GroupKFold on patient_id so no patient appears in both train and test
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import GroupKFold, cross_val_score

visits = pd.read_csv("readmission_visits.csv")  # one row per ER visit, ~6 per patient
X = visits.drop(columns=["patient_id", "readmitted_30d"])
y = visits["readmitted_30d"]

model = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=42)

cv = GroupKFold(n_splits=5)
scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, groups=visits["patient_id"], cv=cv, scoring="roc_auc")
cv_auc = scores.mean()
print(f"5-fold CV AUC: {cv_auc:.2f}")

# Shadow deployment: AUC 0.71 on patients the model had never seen before.
SHADOW_AUC = 0.71
gap = abs(cv_auc - SHADOW_AUC)
assert gap <= 0.05, f"CV AUC {cv_auc:.2f} vs shadow {SHADOW_AUC:.2f}: offline estimate is not honest"
print("offline estimate matches shadow deployment — gate passed")
options.resultLog:
5-fold CV AUC: 0.71
offline estimate matches shadow deployment — gate passed
options.rationale: `GroupKFold` partitions patients, not rows: every visit belonging to a patient lands in the same fold, so each test fold contains only patients absent from training. That recreates the deployment condition — new patient, no memorized history — inside cross-validation. The AUC drops to the honest 0.71, matches shadow mode within tolerance, and the offline estimate finally predicts production behavior instead of flattering it.

hints: A 0.93 that deployment cannot reproduce usually means the validation split is easier than reality. Ask what 'unseen' should mean here: is a row unseen if the model never saw that exact visit — or only if it never saw that patient at all? When several rows describe the same entity, row-level shuffling lets the model meet tomorrow's exam subjects during study hall.

Lines 11–12: `KFold` slices row indices and is completely group-blind — it will even accept a `groups=` argument forwarded by `cross_val_score` and silently ignore it. The visits table has a `patient_id` column that never reaches the splitter. Which splitter family actually consumes group labels?
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
`KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True)` partitions *rows*, but the rows are not independent: each patient contributes around six visits, and many features — age, chronic-condition count, prior-admission history — barely change between a patient's visits. Shuffling scatters those near-duplicate rows across folds, so for almost every test visit the gradient-boosted trees have already trained on other visits from the same patient. The model scores 0.93 by recognizing patients rather than predicting readmission — exactly the ability it loses in deployment, where every patient is new. Hence the honest 0.71 in shadow mode and the 22-point gap that trips the assert on line 19.

### Why the fix is correct
`GroupKFold` with `groups=visits["patient_id"]` partitions *patients*: all of a patient's visits land in the same fold, so each test fold contains only patients the model never trained on. That reproduces the deployment condition inside cross-validation, the AUC falls to its true 0.71, and the gate's tolerance check passes because the offline estimate finally predicts production behavior. The principle: validate at the entity level you deploy at — the splitter must hold out whatever will be genuinely unseen at serving time.
recruiterReview: Sharp catch — the red gate looked like a regression, and you correctly read it as the opposite: an offline estimate inflated by patients straddling the folds. Moving to GroupKFold keyed on patient_id restores the property the gate was built to verify — evaluation at the same granularity as deployment, where every scored patient is genuinely new. I'm equally pleased you didn't take option A's bait; knowing that KFold accepts and silently ignores groups is the difference between using the API and understanding it. Forward-looking: our readmission labels are imbalanced, so consider StratifiedGroupKFold to keep fold prevalence stable, and add a unit test asserting train and test patient sets are disjoint in every fold. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## When Rows Travel in Packs: Grouped Leakage

Cross-validation exists to answer one question: how will the model behave on data it has never seen? Every splitter is a simulation of "never seen", and the simulation is only as honest as your definition of *seen*. A random row-level split defines it as "this exact row was absent from training". That definition quietly collapses whenever rows are not independent — when several rows describe the same patient, customer, speaker, document, sensor, or account. The entity then straddles the split: its test rows are new, but its fingerprint is not.

### Why the metric inflates
Rows that share an entity share signal. A patient's age, chronic conditions, and admission history barely change between visits; a speaker's voice is the same across clips; a customer's spending profile persists across months. A flexible model — gradient boosting, a neural network, even k-NN — learns to recognize these fingerprints. Under a row-level split it gets test questions about entities whose answers it has effectively already read, so the metric measures *recall of the familiar* rather than *generalization to the new*. The score can rise by ten, twenty, even thirty points, and nothing crashes: the bug is purely an inflated number, which is why it routinely survives until a deployment comparison exposes it.

### The deployment question
The cure starts with a question, not an API: **what will be unseen at serving time?** If the deployed model scores patients it has never treated, validation must hold out entire patients. If it scores new stores, hold out stores; new speakers, hold out speakers. The group key is not a modeling preference — it is dictated by how the system will be used. Evaluate at the entity level you deploy at.

### The group-aware toolbox
scikit-learn ships dedicated splitters: `GroupKFold` (each group lands in exactly one test fold), `StratifiedGroupKFold` (group-disjoint folds that also balance label prevalence), `GroupShuffleSplit` (random group holdouts), and `LeaveOneGroupOut`. All of them consume a `groups=` array aligned with your rows. Two sharp edges to remember: plain `KFold` will *accept and silently ignore* `groups` when it arrives via `cross_val_score`, and group splitters produce slightly uneven fold sizes because groups are atomic — the first is a trap, the second is expected.

### How to catch it before the gate does
Run validation both ways. If group-level CV scores meaningfully below row-level CV, that gap *is* the leakage measurement — and the lower number is the one to publish. A disjointness assert inside the fold loop (`set(train_groups).isdisjoint(test_groups)`) turns the property into executable documentation. Best of all is an external reference such as a shadow deployment or a prospectively held-out cohort: an offline estimate that cannot predict that number is not an estimate, it is a hope.

### Common variations of this bug
- Medical imaging: multiple scans or slices of the same patient split across train and test.
- Audio: clips from the same speaker on both sides of a speech or speaker-ID split.
- NLP: paragraphs or sentences from the same document or author scattered across folds.
- Churn and finance: the same customer's monthly snapshots divided by a random row split.
- Vision: augmented copies (crops, flips, rotations) generated *before* the split, so a test image's twin sits in training.
- Geospatial and IoT: readings from the same site, device, or well shared between folds.
